1.synthesis: Moses's third objection — 'They will not believe me or listen to my voice' (v.1) — receives not an argument but three signs. The staff thrown down becomes a serpent; Moses seizes it by the tail and it becomes a staff again (vv.2–4). His hand thrust into his cloak becomes leprous — white as snow — then is restored when thrust in again (vv.6–7). And if these two fail to convince, water from the Nile poured on dry ground will become blood (v.9). The three signs are credentials for Moses's mission and demonstrations of divine power over Egypt's emblems: the serpent-staff counters the Pharaonic uraeus cobra; the blood-from-Nile attacks the river that Pharaoh's empire depends upon. The signs are not ends in themselves but means of establishing that YHWH has appeared to Moses and commissioned him.
1.historical_context: The serpent (nachash) was the preeminent symbol of Egyptian royal power — the uraeus cobra on Pharaoh's crown represented divine protection and kingship. When Aaron later performs this sign before Pharaoh (ch.7), his serpent swallows the Egyptian magicians' serpents: YHWH's power devours Pharaoh's. The leprous hand-sign (vv.6–7) demonstrates instant judgment and instant healing — YHWH controls disease as well as wonder. The water-to-blood sign (v.9) anticipates the first plague (ch.7:20–21) and targets the Nile, Egypt's life source and the river where Israel's sons were drowned. The three signs together constitute a compressed preview of the ten plagues: Egypt's symbols and sources of power are subject to YHWH's command.
1.literary_structure: The sign-sequence follows a graduated structure: first sign (staff/serpent), second sign (leprous hand), then — if neither persuades — third sign (water to blood). The escalation mirrors the later plague structure: initial signs give way to greater demonstrations. Moses' objection in v.1 is about credibility with the Israelites, not about ability; the signs address credibility by providing external confirmation of divine commissioning. The staff that begins as a shepherd's crook ends as the instrument of Exodus wonders — 'the staff of God' (v.20). The mundane shepherd's tool is transformed into the vehicle of divine power, the same logic by which God consistently works: ordinary instruments, extraordinary divine action.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry on the signs: 'God here shows his power to confirm the mission of Moses. Observe how the objections of unbelief are answered by the proofs of divine power … the staff, the familiar tool of his daily work, is the instrument of the first wonder, to show that God needs no extraordinary means, but can do his work by the most ordinary things.' Gill on the staff-to-serpent: 'Moses is bid not to be afraid; he that was to face one of the greatest monarchs in the world must first learn to fear nothing but God, not even a serpent he had just reason to fear.' The recovery of the serpent by the tail — the most dangerous grip — is a lesson in faith: obedience to the divine word overcomes natural fear, and what seemed a threat becomes the instrument of divine authority.
1.christology: The serpent-on-a-pole that Moses later lifted in the wilderness (Numbers 21:8–9) is explicitly applied to Christ by Jesus: 'as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up' (John 3:14). The pattern established here — YHWH's power displayed through a serpent-sign that overcomes death and judgment — points forward to the cross, where the judgment of the world's sin is borne and overcome. The water-to-blood sign previews the first plague and also echoes the Last Supper inversion: at the Exodus, the river that was life became death; at the Table, the wine that was death (the cup of judgment) becomes the blood of the new covenant, the instrument of life.

10.synthesis: Moses's fourth objection reaches the personal level: 'Oh, my Lord, I am not eloquent, either in the past or since you have spoken to your servant, but I am slow of speech and of tongue' (v.10). God's answer cuts through the excuse: 'Who has made man's mouth? Who makes him mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Is it not I, the LORD? Now therefore go, and I will be with your mouth and teach you what you shall speak' (vv.11–12). Moses still resists: 'Oh, my Lord, please send someone else' (v.13). At this point, 'the anger of the LORD was kindled against Moses' — and yet God provides Aaron as the solution rather than withdrawing the commission. Aaron, already on his way, will be Moses's mouth; Moses will be 'as God to him' (v.16). The prophet-pattern is established: God speaks to Moses, Moses speaks to Aaron, Aaron speaks to the people.
10.historical_context: Moses's claim of being 'slow of speech and tongue' has been variously interpreted: a speech impediment, difficulty with Egyptian (his Hebrew superior to his Egyptian after forty years in Midian), or a rhetorical self-description of inadequacy. Stephen in Acts 7:22 says Moses was 'mighty in words and deeds' as a young man — suggesting the issue is self-perception after the wilderness humbling rather than a physiological condition. The anger of YHWH kindled against Moses (v.14) is one of the very few moments in the OT where God expresses anger at a servant while still providing for their failure. Aaron is described as 'the Levite' — his tribal identity is emphasized, anticipating his priestly role. The 'prophet' relationship established in v.16 (Moses as God, Aaron as prophet/mouth) is the template for the prophetic office throughout Israel's history.
10.literary_structure: The four objections of Moses (3:11, 3:13, 4:1, 4:10) and the final plea in 4:13 form a sustained negotiation-reluctance sequence unique to the prophetic call narratives. Jeremiah's call (Jeremiah 1:6: 'I do not know how to speak') and Isaiah's ('Woe is me … I am a man of unclean lips', Isaiah 6:5) follow the same pattern: self-disclosure of inadequacy before divine commissioning. The divine anger in v.14 draws a line: God's patience with Moses's objections has run its course, and yet the provision of Aaron shows that divine anger does not mean divine abandonment. The structural result is that Moses goes forward not with his own eloquence but with God's words transmitted through Aaron — the failure of self becomes the platform for divine speech.
10.theological_application: Gill on 'who hath made man's mouth?': 'God made the mouth and tongue of Moses, as of all men, and could remove all obstructions and impediments in his mouth, and give him power and ability to speak freely, readily, and eloquently.' Matthew Henry: 'If God calls any to public services, he either will qualify them for the service, or will furnish them with those who shall supply their deficiencies … Moses's weakness should be no hindrance to God's glory; the more defective the instrument, the more illustrious would be the divine power.' Paul's 'my speech and my message were not in plausible words of wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power' (1 Corinthians 2:4) is the NT enactment of this principle.
10.christology: The Moses–Aaron prophet-pattern (Moses hears from God, Aaron speaks to the people) anticipates the Christ-apostle pattern: the Word given by God enters the world through a commissioned mouth. But in Christ, the pattern is fulfilled without the Aaron-proxy: Christ himself is both the Word who comes from the Father and the one who speaks it. The 'slow of speech' Moses who resists his commissioning contrasts with the 'Word become flesh' who comes willingly to speak what the Father gives him (John 12:49: 'for I have not spoken on my own authority, but the Father who sent me has himself given me a commandment — what to say and what to speak'). God makes mouths; the Son is the eternal Word that all mouths speak imperfectly and he speaks perfectly.

18.synthesis: Moses takes his leave of Jethro — 'Let me go back to my brothers in Egypt and see whether they are still alive' — and Jethro releases him with blessing (v.18). YHWH reassures Moses that 'all the men who were seeking your life are dead' (v.19 — the Pharaoh who sought to kill him is dead). The theological stakes are raised: Moses is given the firstborn-son language for Israel before Pharaoh: 'Israel is my firstborn son, and I say to you, Let my son go that he may serve me. If you refuse to let him go, behold, I will kill your firstborn son' (vv.22–23). The confrontation will be son against son: YHWH's firstborn Israel against Pharaoh's firstborn Egypt — a conflict that will culminate in the final plague. The strange incident of vv.24–26 — YHWH meeting Moses to kill him, Zipporah circumcising their son to avert it — grounds the Exodus-deliverer himself in the covenant sign of circumcision.
18.historical_context: The declaration of Israel as YHWH's 'firstborn son' (v.22) is the Exodus's central theological framing: Israel has a filial relationship to YHWH that makes their enslavement an affront to the divine Father. The firstborn in ANE culture held the position of supreme honor, inheritance, and representative authority. YHWH's firstborn being enslaved by Pharaoh is analogous to a king's son being held as a slave by a vassal. The tenth plague — death of Egypt's firstborn — is the precise counter-judgment: Pharaoh killed Israel's sons in the Nile (1:22); YHWH will kill Pharaoh's firstborn. The circumcision incident (vv.24–26) has generated extensive debate; the most coherent reading is that Moses had not circumcised his son and was therefore in covenant violation — Zipporah's act removes the violation and averts the divine judgment.
18.literary_structure: The firstborn-son declaration (vv.22–23) stands as the Exodus narrative's theological thesis statement, placed here before Moses even meets Pharaoh: YHWH is claiming Israel as his son and warning that the refusal to release him will cost Pharaoh his own son. Everything that follows in the plague narrative — each exchange with Pharaoh, each escalating sign — unfolds within this declared framework. The circumcision incident (vv.24–26) interrupts the return journey in a startling way, but it serves to establish that the one who will lead the Exodus is himself a circumcised member of the covenant. Moses cannot deliver the covenant people while himself standing outside the covenant sign.
18.theological_application: Gill on Israel as YHWH's firstborn: 'Not that the people of Israel, as a nation, were really the first in time of all the nations of the world, or that they had the preeminence over all others by nature; but they were the first and chief in the love and esteem of God, and whom he chose, and to whom he gave privileges above all others.' Matthew Henry: 'God calls Israel his son, his firstborn … the relation they stood in to him, children by adoption and grace, firstborn, having the privileges of primogeniture, the birthright-blessing. This is the first time that Israel is called the Son of God, the beginning of that divine sonship which the NT reveals as fulfilled in Christ.' The threat to Pharaoh's firstborn is covenant justice: Israel, God's son, held captive; Egypt's firstborn, the cost of refusal.
18.christology: 'Israel is my firstborn son' (v.22) is the Exodus's christological seed. Hosea 11:1 — 'out of Egypt I called my son' — cites this language for the Exodus and Matthew 2:15 applies it to Christ's return from Egypt as a child. The Exodus is not merely historical but typological: as Israel (God's son) came out of Egypt, so Christ (God's Son) came out of Egypt. The firstborn-son language transfers from the nation to the individual who embodies the nation: Christ is the true firstborn over all creation (Colossians 1:15), the one in whom the Exodus sonship is fulfilled. The tenth plague — death of firstborn — is averted for Israel by the Passover lamb's blood; it is averted for the world by the blood of God's own firstborn at the cross.

27.synthesis: Aaron, sent by God into the wilderness, meets Moses at the mountain of God — the bush itself becomes the meeting place (v.27). Moses tells Aaron everything YHWH has sent him to speak and all the signs he has commanded (v.28). They gather the elders of Israel; Aaron speaks the words, Moses performs the signs. The Israelites believe; and when they hear that YHWH has visited them and seen their affliction, 'they bowed their heads and worshiped' (v.31). This first Israelite worship response — before any plague, before any Passover, before the Sinai covenant — is the pure response of a people who have heard that their God has not forgotten them. The believing and the bowing are the appropriate responses to the news that God has seen their suffering and is acting.
27.historical_context: The gathering of the elders of Israel (v.29) is the formal civic assembly of the covenant community's representative leadership. The elders will be present at the first Passover (12:21), will accompany Moses up Sinai (24:1, 9), and will function as the representative body of the people throughout the wilderness years. The sequence of Aaron speaking (v.30) then Moses performing the signs (v.30) enacts the prophet-pattern established in v.16: Aaron as mouth, Moses as God-representative. The people's response — believing (v.31a), hearing (v.31b), bowing and worshiping (v.31c) — is the OT pattern of covenant faith: hearing the divine word, trusting it, and responding in worship. The worship here will be severely tested by Pharaoh's hardening, Moses's own crisis of faith (5:22–23), and the long ordeal of the plagues.
27.literary_structure: The meeting of Moses and Aaron, the reporting, the assembly of the elders, and the people's worship form a brief but structurally complete unit that resolves the commission-objection sequence of chs.3–4. Moses objected that the people would not believe; they believe. Moses objected about his speech; Aaron speaks. The signs are performed and function as intended. The unit closes with a worship response that prepares the ground for Pharaoh's refusal — the people's faith will be tested immediately, and their worship will give way to complaint in 5:21. But the pattern of faith-before-deliverance is established: Israel worships in response to the promise before the promise is fulfilled.
27.theological_application: Gill on the elders' belief: 'they all gave credit to Moses and Aaron, believed that they were sent of God to them; and they were the more induced to this, by the signs shown unto them … and that YHWH had visited the children of Israel and had looked upon their affliction; this was what they had long wished for and waited for.' Matthew Henry: 'The people received the tidings with joy and thankfulness … when they heard that God had visited his people, they worshiped. We must meet God's gracious visits with our humble adorations.' The bowing and worshiping in v.31 is the right posture before news of divine grace: not argument, not analysis, but worship. The people will often fail to maintain this posture, but the pattern is laid down at the beginning of the Exodus.
27.christology: 'They bowed their heads and worshiped' (v.31) — this response to the announcement that YHWH has 'visited' his people and 'seen their affliction' is the Exodus's first doxological moment. The same word for divine 'visit' (pāqad) that Joseph used — 'God will surely visit you' (Genesis 50:24) — is now enacted: God has come. The NT describes the incarnation as the ultimate divine visitation: Zechariah's Benedictus (Luke 1:68) opens with 'Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people' — the Exodus vocabulary applied to Christ's birth. The Exodus pattern is: first the promise heard → believing → worship; then the deliverance enacted. The same pattern holds in the gospel: the announcement of the kingdom calls for faith and worship before the full redemption is seen.
